question.text: Scenario: Mr. Arvind Sharma, aged 42, is a senior manager at an IT company earning Rs. 18 lakh per annum (gross). His wife Mrs. Priya works part-time earning Rs. 6 lakh per annum. Their combined monthly expenses are Rs. 1,20,000. They have two children aged 14 and 10. Arvind's current investments include: Equity Mutual Funds Rs. 35 lakh, PPF Rs. 12 lakh (current balance), Bank Fixed Deposits Rs. 8 lakh, Employee PF balance Rs. 18 lakh, and a savings bank balance of Rs. 3 lakh. He has an outstanding home loan of Rs. 22 lakh with EMI of Rs. 25,000/month (included in expenses above) and no other loans. His employer contributes Rs. 1,800/month to PF and he contributes the same. He wants to retire at age 58 and needs a retirement corpus of Rs. 3 crore. He also needs Rs. 30 lakh for his elder child's education in 4 years and Rs. 25 lakh for the younger child in 8 years. Assume equity returns at 12% CAGR and debt returns at 7% CAGR. Inflation is 6% p.a. Question 4 of 5: To accumulate Rs. 30 lakh for the elder child's education in 4 years, how much lump sum should Arvind invest today at an expected return of 10% p.a.?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** PV = FV / (1+r)^n = 30,00,000 / (1.10)^4 = 30,00,000 / 1.4641 = Rs. 20,49,040. **The Trap:** Rs. 22,89,134 uses 7% discount rate instead of 10%. Rs. 25,00,000 is a rough estimate without proper discounting. **Cross-Exam Context:** Goal-based PV calculations are central to X-A and XVII (Retirement Adviser). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For a 4-year education goal with moderate risk, use a balanced allocation (say 60:40 equity:debt) which gives ~10% expected return — pure equity is too risky for this timeline.
